results.possibleIdentity: Kira Gale (1941–2018) was a multi-disciplinary artist, experimental filmmaker, and historian who was active in the New York City art and technology scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Born in Washington, D.C., and later based in Omaha, Nebraska, she began her career as an artist focusing on experimental media. During the early 1970s in NYC, she was a filmmaker who specialized in Super-8 film, specifically experimenting with technical processes such as solarization and home processing of film. Her work in this period was recognized in publications like 'Radical Software' (1970), a foundational journal for the alternative media and video art movement, where she was listed as a contributor or contact. This work in experimental media and her presence in the NYC art/tech community align with the activities of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Gale later transitioned into a career as a historian and author, becoming a prominent authority on the Lewis and Clark expedition and the history of Omaha. Her artistic background continued through photography, notably in her 1987 exhibit 'Old Jules Country,' which paired images of the Nebraska Sandhills with the writing of Mari Sandoz. She authored several historical books, including 'The Death of Meriwether Lewis: A Historic Crime Scene Investigation' and 'Lewis and Clark Road Trips.'
